version: Curated philosophical scaffolds for AI-deployment reasoning

utilitarian.core_question: Of the available options, which produces the greatest aggregate well-being across all affected parties — accounting for probabilities, magnitudes, and time horizons?
analytical_steps: Enumerate every affected party (direct users, third parties, future users, the operator).
For each option (deploy / deploy-with-controls / don't deploy / alternative), forecast the distribution of outcomes.
Weight each outcome by probability and magnitude of welfare change.
Sum across parties and compare options.
Identify the option with the highest expected aggregate welfare and the assumptions it depends on.
key_considerations: Avoid privileging present users over future or downstream parties.
Distinguish expected value from worst-case value — utilitarian reasoning under risk often misses tail harms.
Watch for measurement bias: easily quantified benefits can crowd out hard-to-measure harms.
Be explicit about whose welfare you are counting and at what discount rate.
typical_failure_modes: Justifying serious harm to a minority by aggregate gain to a majority.
Reasoning past constraint-style obligations (e.g., consent) by netting them out.

deontological.core_question: Independent of consequences, what duties, rights, and constraints apply — and does each option respect persons as ends in themselves rather than mere means?
analytical_steps: Identify duties owed to each affected party (consent, truth-telling, non-coercion, due process).
Identify the rights each party can claim against the deploying organization.
Apply the universalizability test: could this practice be coherently willed as a general policy?
Apply the humanity formula: are affected persons being treated merely as data sources or processing targets?
Identify any duty or right that would be violated and ask whether a redesign could remove that violation.
key_considerations: Constraints are typically not weighable against aggregate utility.
Informed consent is central; opaque automated decisions are suspect by default.
Procedural fairness matters even when outcomes are 'better' in aggregate.
Duties of transparency increase with the stakes of the decision the system informs.
typical_failure_modes: Treating absence of complaints as presence of consent.
Reducing 'transparency' to a policy document nobody reads.
Allowing the system to make decisions whose appeal path is theoretical.

virtue_ethics.core_question: What would a person of good practical wisdom — and what would an organization of good character — actually do here, and what does habitually deploying systems like this make us into?
analytical_steps: Identify the relevant virtues for the practice (honesty, justice, prudence, courage, humility, care).
Identify the vices the system or its deployment context could encourage (sloppiness, deference, evasion of responsibility).
Ask what an experienced, conscientious practitioner in this domain would recognize as the right way to act.
Consider what habits this deployment will form in the operators and downstream users.
Identify whether the system supports or substitutes for the human judgment that the role requires.
key_considerations: Character formation is a real organizational outcome — not a side effect.
Over-reliance on automated systems can erode skills that the role needs preserved.
Virtue-ethical analysis is especially useful when consequences are uncertain.
Ask who, concretely, is responsible — not just which committee.
typical_failure_modes: Treating 'human in the loop' as virtue when the human cannot meaningfully override.
Letting metrics displace judgment in a role that exists because judgment was needed.

care_ethics.tradition: Feminist ethics of care (Gilligan, Noddings, Held)
care_ethics.core_question: Who is in a relationship of dependence on this system, who is most vulnerable, and what does responsiveness to their concrete situation require?
analytical_steps: Map the network of relationships the system embeds in.
Identify the most vulnerable affected parties (least power, least exit, least recourse).
Ask what those parties would actually need (not what the deployer wants to provide).
Examine the existing relationships the system risks displacing.
Decide what concrete responsiveness obligations the deployer takes on.
key_considerations: Vulnerability is asymmetric — design and process must compensate.
Care responsibilities don't disappear when a process is automated; they migrate.
Voice and exit are not interchangeable. Both matter.
Generic 'feedback channels' rarely substitute for designed responsiveness.
typical_failure_modes: Designing for the median user and shedding edge cases.
Treating the absence of escalation as evidence of acceptance.

rawlsian.core_question: If you did not know which affected party you would turn out to be, would you accept this deployment — and does the worst-off party have a justified complaint?
analytical_steps: Apply the original position: reason as if you might be any affected party, with no knowledge of which.
Identify the least-advantaged party.
Ask whether the deployment improves or worsens their position.
Distinguish inequalities that pass the difference principle (benefit the least advantaged) from those that don't.
Identify procedural-justice failures: voice, due process, ability to contest.
key_considerations: Aggregate gains do not justify making the worst-off worse off.
Procedural justice matters independently of distribution.
The veil of ignorance is a check on bias — including organizational bias toward the easy case.
Consider what the system makes harder for already-disadvantaged parties.
typical_failure_modes: Confusing 'rising tide' arguments with justice arguments.
Defining the affected class narrowly enough that the worst-off don't appear in it.
Treating efficient outcomes as fair outcomes.
